kept.text:
How to actually use "debt" for hyper growth.

For us, it has contributed to working with a $50B+ portfolio, and for our clients generate 300B+ impressions.

When you hear the word "debt," you immediately think about owing money.

However, that is not the case here.

It’s strategic debt, placing value into the network without demanding immediate return.

No invoice. No expectation. No clock.

Just deliberate deposits into relationships, ecosystems, and moments that compound over time.

Because when distribution becomes free, reputation becomes the only asset that matters.

And reputation doesn’t scale through transactions.

It scales through asymmetric generosity.

Being a bootstrapped company, we have had to grow atypically. We have never run paid ads or done cold outreach.

Our only growth has come from network effects.

Think about it.

If creating content is essentially free and you can create unlimited amounts, the only thing you have left is your reputation.

Instead of viewing business as a zero-sum game, think of it as a positive-sum system.

Rather than trying to take someone else’s slice of the pie, focus on making the pie bigger.

We invest into the present so the future has no choice but to return value.

That could mean helping a founder with no immediate commercial upside.

It could mean making a high-quality introduction without asking to be compensated.

It could mean supporting an ecosystem before it is obvious.

It could mean hosting a dinner where the immediate ROI is unclear, but the room is full of people who may shape the next decade.

It could mean giving a client, partner, investor, or creator a level of insight they did not pay for, because you know the relationship will compound.

The mistake most companies make is they only take action when there is a visible transaction attached.

This mindset forces you to reassess opportunities, partnerships, and decisions through a different lens.

You stop asking “what do we get now,” and start asking “what does this unlock later.”

That’s when the game changes.

The win often comes in the form of luck, but luck is manufactured.

Strategic debt only works when it’s applied consistently, across the entire company.

Every employee.

Every interaction.

Every decision.

By performing these actions thousands of times over months and years, you don’t just grow.

You become embedded.

More connected.
More trusted.
More relevant.

So the next time you assess ROI, whether it's a campaign, a hire, a partnership, or your time, don’t judge it purely by immediate outcomes.

Instead ask:

What does this compound into?
What future door does this open?
What reputation does this reinforce?
What network does this give us access to?

Luck is the result of accumulated trust, repeated exposure, and well-placed leverage over long periods of time.

This is where most people get it wrong.

They treat actions as isolated.

The best operators treat them as connected.

A single interaction doesn’t matter.

A thousand aligned interactions do.

That’s the game.

Do not optimize for individual wins.

Companies don’t scale because of one decision.

They scale because thousands of small actions compound in the same direction.

When a team understands that, behavior changes.

Short-term tradeoffs become obvious.

Reputation becomes protected.

Opportunities compound faster than they can be tracked.

And over time, the market starts returning everything that was planted.

quoted_tweet.text:
I left Google DeepMind, moved from SF to NYC, all within 2 weeks to join @quadrillion_ai — to build the future of automated research intelligence with the highest slope founder and most talent dense team. 

I grew up in Silicon Valley — the old Facebook office was my second home. I’d hang out there after school, drawing with my crayons while looking around at the sea of computers with lines of code. Since a young age, I felt empowered to have an array of interests beyond tech: piano, ballet, figure skating, art. The valley embraced diversity of thought, and that’s what inspired me to stay for Stanford and my career thus far. 

But today, SF is one big hive-mind.

So, I moved to NYC, away from family and friends to build a company that doesn’t need to rely on a bubble to survive.

I’m meeting customers day after day in all kinds of verticals, connecting with them in different ways and seeing our product bring real value. Here, I’m able to live in diversity of thought.

I’m excited to build the future of research in the city of opportunity. Let’s chat if this excites you.

profile_bio.description: building the future of automated research intelligence @quadrillion_ai | prev @GoogleDeepMind, @scale_AI, @stanford

kept.text: I find it odd that among pgvector, Clickhouse, Snowflake, and Databricks, only the 1st of those 4 supports elementwise arithmetic for vectors (e.g. vec1 + vec2). It's actually extremely useful.

profile_bio.description: UX Designer Internal Tools @citi | Prev: Risk Manager @AlamedaResearch, QA Engineer at Knight Capital Group | he him pronouns in bio

quoted_tweet.text:
E.g. today I learned they added 6 new vector functions sometime in the last 6 or so months since I last checked. I have over a dozen custom Python UDFs for vector operations*, and the new functions only replace two of them (VECTOR_AVG and VECTOR_SUM).

Still no HNSW indexing for ANN btw, which is the most useful aspect of any vector db.

In something like pgvector, all of my UDFs would be unnecessary, they're either directly supported operations or they're trivially calculable from basic operations. (E.g. weighted average function, stddev, etc.)

What's insulting about this is Snowflake introduced vector support in July 2024 and highly advertised it as part of their AI/ML push, then they'd have their salespeople go around advertising that Snowflake has vector support! Except back then you only had 4 whole functions and could barely do anything natively except distance calculations.

So in the last 2 years of having this product feature, they've barely updated it, it still hardly does anything, and they still haven't added what would be the most critical feature (HNSW indexing). They made a big deal about releasing it and then let it rot in the corner once their sales team was equipped to say they technically had the feature, barren as it is.

I'm picking on Snowflake's vector support but there are dozens of other parts of Snowflake's product line up that feel exactly like this: they rushed to get something out that is fundamentally feature incomplete and then the process of maintaining it is almost completely abandoned.

What appears to be happening from the outside is twofold. First, the company seems to be driven by product managers and salespeople, and the engineers as well-comped as they are, basically have no say; I doubt these engineers enjoy letting their work get shoved into the corner and I'm sure they're well aware of all the feature gaps. Second, middle managers at Snowflake seem to get a lot of credit and reward for releasing new things but nobody gets any credit for making things better or actually usable.

As someone who's used Snowflake for a while (albeit I've reduced a lot of dependency on Snowflake over the past year, thank you Clickhouse and Iceberg), it drives me crazy. Everything in the Snowflake feels like this! It's hard to find people deep enough in the weeds of Snowflake to complain to about this. So I just suffer in silence as I navigate around an ecosystem of half-complete products from a team of 9,000 employees and a $50b market cap lol.

Absolutely maddening product. There's no reason it can't be good, they just don't have the internal political ecosystem to foster high quality deliverables.

profile_bio.description: UX Designer Internal Tools @citi | Prev: Risk Manager @AlamedaResearch, QA Engineer at Knight Capital Group | he him pronouns in bio

kept.text:
110% hike at Architect position

Yes, you read that right.

Receiving this message from my very talented student, Sablok made my day.

From starting in ETL to now joining Zensar Technologies as a Databricks Solution Architect, his journey is nothing short of inspiring.

He cracked 5 offers which reflects his strong preparation and in-depth knowledge.

Not waiting for opportunities, he created one for him by choosing Data Engineering domain to take his career ahead.

I feel so proud of you Sablok, seeing the way you evolved over years and reached this height.

Your journey is a message for many to reflect back on their career, domain, skills and take a conscious call towards upskilling.

DM to know more about my Data Engineering Program!

kept.text:
📊 運用データベースの常識が変わります！

従来のOLTPデータベースが抱える課題と、その解決策をまとめました👇
▶︎https://t.co/XDihPxiGz0

🔹 OLTPの役割
決済処理や在庫管理など、リアルタイム取引の中核を担うシステムです。ACID保証・ミリ秒単位の低レイテンシで日常業務を支えていますが、分析用のOLAPとは設計思想が根本的に異なります。

🔹 従来型の限界
非構造化データやベクトル検索・AIワークロードへの対応が不十分です。OLAPとの間に遅いETLパイプラインが必要で、リアルタイムAI活用の大きな壁になっています。厳格なスキーマや垂直スケーリングの限界も深刻です。

🔹 Lakebaseという解答
Databricksが提供する新アーキテクチャ「Lakebase」は、ストレージとコンピューティングを分離し、サーバーレスPostgresとオープン形式でマルチクラウドに対応します。OLTPとOLAPを単一プラットフォームで統合し、AIエージェントや不正検出にほぼリアルタイムでデータを届けます。

💡 つまり「運用DB × 分析基盤 × AI」が一つになる時代の到来です！

#Databricks #データベース #AI
